napkin_math: Original: 224×224×64×4B = 12.25 MB. | im2col: 576 × 49,284 × 4B ≈ 110 MB. | Expansion factor ≈ $k^2 = 9\times$. | For 128 channels: 220 MB just for one layer's im2col buffer.

title: Flash Attention Tiling Strategy
scenario: Standard self-attention materializes the full $S \times S$ attention score matrix in HBM. Flash Attention avoids this. Explain the core systems insight behind Flash Attention's speedup — why does tiling the attention computation into SRAM-sized blocks fundamentally change the bottleneck?
common_mistake: Saying Flash Attention reduces FLOPs. It actually does MORE FLOPs (recomputing softmax statistics) but is faster because it eliminates massive HBM reads/writes.
realistic_solution: Flash Attention exploits the GPU memory hierarchy. Standard attention writes the $S \times S$ matrix (potentially GBs) to HBM, then reads it back for softmax and value multiplication. Flash Attention tiles Q, K, V into blocks that fit in SRAM (~20 MB on A100), computes attention within each tile, and accumulates output without ever materializing the full score matrix in HBM. It trades extra FLOPs (recomputing softmax normalization) for dramatically reduced HBM traffic. Since attention is memory-bandwidth-bound, reducing HBM I/O dominates the runtime.
napkin_math: Standard attention at 4K seq: writes 4096² × 2B × 32 heads = 1 GB to HBM, reads it back = 2 GB total I/O. | Flash Attention: all intermediate data stays in 20 MB SRAM. HBM I/O drops to Q+K+V+O only: ~4 × 4096 × 4096 × 2B = 128 MB. | 15× less HBM traffic → 2-4× wall-clock speedup.

title: MoE Expert Load Imbalance Stall
scenario: You are training a 16-expert MoE model across 16 GPUs. The profiler shows that 14 GPUs finish their expert computation in 2ms, but 2 GPUs consistently take 8ms, creating a 6ms stall at every MoE layer's synchronization barrier. What is the most likely root cause, and what is the throughput impact?
common_mistake: Blaming hardware heterogeneity or slow interconnect. The key insight is that expert routing is learned and can become highly skewed, concentrating tokens on a few experts.
realistic_solution: Expert load imbalance from the routing function. The gating network is routing disproportionately many tokens to 2 'popular' experts, overloading those GPUs. With 16 experts and uniform routing, each GPU should process 1/16 of tokens. If 2 experts receive 4× their fair share, they take 4× longer. The throughput impact: effective MoE layer time is 8ms (max of all GPUs) instead of 2ms — a 4× slowdown on this layer. Mitigation: add a load-balancing auxiliary loss, or use capacity factors to cap per-expert token count.
napkin_math: Uniform: each expert gets 4096/16 = 256 tokens → 2ms. | Skewed: 2 experts get ~1024 tokens → 8ms. | Stall: 16 GPUs wait for the slowest → 4× throughput loss on MoE layers. | If MoE layers are 40% of total step time: overall 2.4× slowdown.

title: Prefill vs Decode Compute Profile
scenario: During LLM inference, the prefill phase processes the entire prompt and the decode phase generates tokens one at a time. Explain why prefill is compute-bound while decode is memory-bandwidth-bound, using arithmetic intensity as the framework.
common_mistake: Thinking both phases have the same bottleneck because they run the same model architecture. The key difference is the effective batch dimension: prefill processes S tokens simultaneously while decode processes 1 token at a time.
realistic_solution: Prefill: processes all $S$ prompt tokens in one batched matrix multiply. For a weight matrix $W$ of size $d \times d$, it computes $X_{S \times d} \cdot W_{d \times d}$ — each weight is reused $S$ times. Arithmetic intensity: $2Sd^2 / (S \cdot d + d^2) \cdot$ bytes $\approx 2d / (1 + d/S)$ FLOPs/byte — very high for large $S$, compute-bound. Decode: processes 1 token, computing $x_{1 \times d} \cdot W_{d \times d}$ — a matrix-vector product. Each weight is used exactly once. AI: $2d^2 / (d + d^2)$ bytes $\approx 2$ FLOPs/byte — deeply memory-bound.
napkin_math: Prefill (S=2048, d=4096): AI ≈ 2×2048×4096² / (2048×4096 + 4096²) × (1/2B) ≈ ~1000 FLOPs/byte → compute-bound. | Decode (S=1): AI ≈ 2 FLOPs/byte → memory-bound. | A100 ridge point: ~250 FLOPs/byte.

title: GQA vs MQA KV Cache Tradeoff
scenario: You are evaluating three attention variants for a new 30B LLM: Multi-Head Attention (MHA, 32 KV heads), Grouped-Query Attention (GQA, 8 KV heads), and Multi-Query Attention (MQA, 1 KV head). Compare the KV cache size and serving throughput implications.
common_mistake: Only comparing memory savings without considering the quality vs throughput tradeoff. MQA saves the most memory but may degrade quality more than GQA, which is why most production models use GQA as a compromise.
realistic_solution: KV cache scales linearly with the number of KV heads. MHA (32 heads): baseline KV cache. GQA (8 heads): 4× smaller KV cache. MQA (1 head): 32× smaller. For serving, KV cache is often the limiting factor for batch size. Smaller KV cache → more concurrent requests → higher throughput. GQA at 8 heads gives 4× the batch size (or 4× the context length) with minimal quality loss, making it the dominant production choice.
napkin_math: 30B model, 60 layers, d_head=128, BF16: | MHA KV per token: 2 × 32 × 128 × 2B × 60 = 960 KB. | GQA (8 heads): 240 KB — 4× smaller. | MQA (1 head): 30 KB — 32× smaller. | On 80GB A100 after weights (60GB): MHA fits ~20 concurrent 4K requests; GQA fits ~83.

title: SSM vs Transformer Hardware Tradeoff
scenario: State-space models (SSMs) like Mamba replace attention with a recurrent scan operation. From a systems perspective, compare the training and inference hardware profiles of SSMs vs Transformers. When does each architecture have a systems advantage?
common_mistake: Assuming SSMs are universally faster. During training, the parallel scan in SSMs maps well to GPUs but lacks the highly optimized GEMM utilization of transformer attention. During inference, SSMs have clear advantages for long sequences because they avoid the KV cache entirely.
realistic_solution: Training: Transformers are compute-bound with excellent GPU utilization (large GEMMs on Tensor Cores). SSMs use parallel scans that are less GEMM-friendly, often achieving lower hardware utilization. Transformers win on training throughput per FLOP. Inference: Transformers have $O(S)$ KV cache memory and must read it every token (bandwidth-bound). SSMs maintain a fixed-size hidden state ($O(d)$) regardless of sequence length — no KV cache, constant memory per token. For long contexts (32K+ tokens), SSMs have a massive serving advantage: constant memory vs linear growth.
napkin_math: 70B Transformer at 32K context: KV cache ≈ 10+ GB per request → limits batch size. | 70B SSM: fixed state ≈ 10 MB per request → 1000× less per-request memory. | Training: Transformer MFU 40-50% (optimized GEMMs). SSM MFU 25-35% (scan ops).

title: Transformer Layer FLOP Decomposition
scenario: For a single transformer layer with hidden dimension $d = 4096$, $h = 32$ heads, head dimension 128, FFN inner dimension $4d = 16384$, and sequence length $S = 2048$, decompose the total FLOPs into attention and FFN components. Which dominates?
common_mistake: Assuming attention dominates because it's the 'novel' part of the transformer. For typical models, the FFN (two large matmuls with 4× expansion) accounts for roughly 2/3 of total FLOPs.
realistic_solution: Attention: Q/K/V projections = $3 \times 2Sd^2 = 6Sd^2$. Score computation = $2S^2 d$. Output projection = $2Sd^2$. Total: $8Sd^2 + 2S^2 d$. FFN: Two linear layers ($d \to 4d$ and $4d \to d$): $2 \times 2S \cdot d \cdot 4d = 16Sd^2$. For $S=2048, d=4096$: Attention ≈ $8 \times 2048 \times 4096^2 + 2 \times 2048^2 \times 4096 \approx 275$B + 34B = 309B FLOPs. FFN ≈ $16 \times 2048 \times 4096^2 \approx 550$B FLOPs. FFN is ~64% of total.
napkin_math: Attention projections: $8Sd^2 = 8 \times 2K \times 16M \approx 275B$. | Attention scores: $2S^2d = 2 \times 4M \times 4K \approx 34B$. | FFN: $16Sd^2 \approx 550B$. | Total per layer: ~860B FLOPs. | FFN dominates at ~64%.

title: DLRM Embedding Table Sharding Strategy
scenario: A production DLRM has 50 embedding tables ranging from 1M to 500M rows, with total embedding memory of 400 GB. You have 8 GPUs with 80 GB HBM each (640 GB total). Design a sharding strategy. Why can't you simply shard each table evenly across all GPUs?
common_mistake: Assuming row-parallel sharding (splitting each table across GPUs) is always best. This creates massive AllToAll communication because every lookup requires cross-GPU communication for every table.
realistic_solution: Use table-wise sharding as the primary strategy: assign entire tables to individual GPUs, balancing total memory per GPU. For tables that exceed single-GPU capacity (e.g., a 100 GB table), use row-wise sharding for those specific tables only. The key insight: table-wise sharding minimizes AllToAll communication because lookups to a table stay on one GPU. Row-wise sharding requires gathering partial embeddings from all GPUs for every lookup. Mixed sharding (table-wise + row-wise for oversized tables) is the production approach used in Meta's DLRM infrastructure.
napkin_math: 50 tables across 8 GPUs: average ~50 GB per GPU (within 80 GB). | Table-wise: only cross-table feature interaction needs AllToAll. | Row-wise on all tables: 50 × batch_size lookups × 7/8 remote = massive AllToAll. | Communication ratio: table-wise ~10× less AllToAll volume than full row-wise.

title: MoE Sparse vs Dense FLOP Equivalence
scenario: Mixtral 8x7B has 8 experts of ~7B parameters each with top-2 routing, for a total of ~47B parameters. However, each token only activates 2 of 8 experts. Compare the per-token inference FLOPs to a dense 47B model and a dense 13B model. Why does MoE achieve 'more capacity for fewer FLOPs'?
common_mistake: Thinking Mixtral uses 47B parameters per token. The '8x7B' naming is misleading — the shared attention layers mean total unique parameters are ~47B, but per-token active parameters are ~13B.
realistic_solution: Each token activates: shared attention layers (~6B params) + 2 of 8 FFN experts (~7B each, but each expert FFN is ~3.5B). Active params per token: ~13B. Per-token FLOPs are approximately equal to a dense 13B model, NOT a 47B model. But the model has 47B total parameters — more representational capacity (different inputs route to different experts, allowing specialization). The systems tradeoff: MoE gets 47B-quality at 13B-compute, but requires 47B of memory and introduces routing + AllToAll communication overhead.
napkin_math: Dense 47B: ~94 TFLOPs per token (at ~2 FLOPs/param). | MoE 8x7B (top-2): ~26 TFLOPs per token (13B active). | Dense 13B: ~26 TFLOPs. | MoE compute ≈ dense 13B, but memory ≈ dense 47B. | Memory: 47B × 2B (BF16) = 94 GB vs 26 GB for dense 13B.

title: Attention GPU Utilization Drop at Long Sequences
scenario: Your LLM serving system shows 55% GPU compute utilization during prefill for short prompts (256 tokens) but drops to 25% for long prompts (16K tokens), even though long prompts have MORE total FLOPs. The batch size is the same in both cases. Diagnose the root cause.
common_mistake: Thinking more FLOPs should always mean higher utilization. The issue is that long-sequence attention shifts the bottleneck from compute to memory.
realistic_solution: At short sequences, the FFN layers (large GEMMs) dominate the FLOP mix, and these are compute-bound with high utilization. At 16K tokens, the quadratic attention score matrix ($S^2$) grows 64× ($16K^2 / 256^2 = 3906\times$) while FFN only grows linearly. The attention computation must materialize and read back a massive score matrix from HBM, becoming memory-bandwidth-bound. The overall workload shifts from compute-dominated (high utilization) to bandwidth-dominated (low utilization). Solution: use Flash Attention to keep attention tiled in SRAM, or disaggregate prefill to specialized hardware
napkin_math: At 256 tokens: attention scores = $256^2 × 32$ heads × 2B = 4 MB (trivial). FFN dominates → compute-bound. | At 16K: attention scores = $16K^2 × 32 × 2B$ = 16 GB (!). Must read/write from HBM. | HBM becomes the bottleneck → utilization drops.

title: CNN Translation Equivariance as Hardware Efficiency
scenario: A vision transformer (ViT) and a CNN both achieve similar accuracy on ImageNet. However, the CNN requires 4× fewer parameters and 3× fewer FLOPs. From a systems perspective, explain why the CNN's inductive bias (translation equivariance and local connectivity) translates directly into hardware efficiency gains.
common_mistake: Explaining equivariance purely from a statistical learning perspective without connecting it to concrete hardware benefits: weight sharing, regular memory access patterns, and data reuse.
realistic_solution: Translation equivariance means the same convolution kernel is shared across all spatial positions. Systems implications: (1) Parameter efficiency: a 3×3×256×256 conv has only 590K parameters but covers the entire spatial resolution — ViT needs position-specific parameters for each patch. (2) Compute regularity: convolutions map perfectly to systolic arrays and Tensor Cores due to their regular, predictable access patterns. (3) Spatial locality: each conv only reads a local receptive field, enabling excellent cache utilization. (4) Data reuse: each weight is reused across all spatial positions,
napkin_math: CNN (ResNet-50): 25M params, 4 GFLOPs for 224×224. | ViT-B/16: 86M params, 17 GFLOPs for 224×224. | Param ratio: 3.4×. FLOP ratio: 4.25×. | CNN weight reuse: each 3×3 kernel used 56×56 = 3136 times at one resolution.

title: MoE Capacity Factor Tuning
scenario: You are tuning the capacity factor (CF) for a 64-expert MoE model. CF=1.0 means each expert can handle exactly $B/E$ tokens (batch size / num experts). Your monitoring shows 5% of tokens are being dropped at CF=1.0. Increasing CF to 1.5 eliminates drops but increases per-expert buffer memory by 50% and slows AllToAll. How do you reason about the optimal CF?
common_mistake: Setting CF very high 'just to be safe.' This wastes memory (each expert pre-allocates $CF \times B/E$ token buffers) and increases AllToAll volume because more tokens are dispatched.
realistic_solution: The capacity factor controls a three-way tradeoff: (1) Quality: dropped tokens hurt model quality (they get a zero or residual-only path). At 5% drop rate, quality loss may be significant. (2) Memory: buffer memory per expert = $CF \times B/E \times d \times$ bytes. Going from CF=1.0 to 1.5 adds 50% memory overhead per expert. (3) Communication: AllToAll volume scales with the actual number of dispatched tokens, which increases with CF. Optimal approach: profile the actual routing distribution. If it's near-uniform (low variance), CF=1.1-1.2 suffices. If heavily skewed (some experts are 'popul
napkin_math: 64 experts, batch 8192, d=4096, BF16: | CF=1.0: buffer per expert = 128 tokens × 4096 × 2B = 1 MB. Total: 64 MB. | CF=1.5: 192 tokens × 4096 × 2B = 1.5 MB. Total: 96 MB (+32 MB). | CF=1.25: 160 tokens, total 80 MB — good compromise.

title: Transformer Architecture Enables Tensor Parallelism
scenario: Explain why the transformer architecture is particularly well-suited to tensor parallelism. Specifically, how do the multi-head attention and FFN layers naturally decompose across GPUs, and where are the synchronization points?
common_mistake: Thinking tensor parallelism requires splitting every operation. The key insight is that attention heads are independent and the FFN has a natural column-then-row split.
realistic_solution: Multi-head attention: each head computes Q, K, V projections and attention independently. With 32 heads across 4 GPUs, each GPU computes 8 heads with zero cross-GPU communication until the output projection. The output projection requires an AllReduce to combine partial results. FFN layer: the first linear ($d \to 4d$) is column-parallel (each GPU holds $d \to d$ slice). The second linear ($4d \to d$) is row-parallel. Only one AllReduce is needed after the second linear. Total: 2 AllReduces per transformer layer (one after attention output, one after FFN). This structure is why transformers sc
napkin_math: 4-way TP on 70B model: 2 AllReduces per layer × 80 layers = 160 AllReduces per forward pass. | Each AllReduce on 4 GPUs with NVLink (600 GB/s): ~20 µs for d=4096 vector. | Total sync overhead: ~3.2 ms per forward pass — small vs ~50ms compute.

title: Sparse Attention Patterns Meet Hardware Reality
scenario: A research paper proposes a sparse attention pattern where each token attends to only $\sqrt{S}$ other tokens, reducing theoretical FLOPs from $O(S^2)$ to $O(S^{1.5})$. However, your CUDA benchmark shows only a 1.3× speedup vs Flash Attention at $S=8K$. Why does the theoretical FLOP reduction not translate to proportional wall-clock speedup?
common_mistake: Assuming FLOPs directly predict wall-clock time. On GPUs, irregular memory access patterns from sparse attention can cause worse utilization than dense attention with hardware-optimized kernels.
realistic_solution: Three factors: (1) Memory access irregularity: sparse attention requires gathering non-contiguous K, V entries based on the sparsity pattern. This creates random memory accesses that defeat GPU memory coalescing, reducing effective bandwidth. (2) Warp divergence: different tokens attend to different subsets, so threads within a warp execute different control flow, reducing SIMD efficiency. (3) Kernel optimization gap: Flash Attention is exquisitely optimized for dense attention with tiled SRAM usage. Sparse attention kernels lack this level of optimization and can't exploit the same regular ti
napkin_math: Theoretical FLOPs: $S^{1.5}/S^2 = 1/\sqrt{S}$. At S=8K: $1/90 \approx 0.011$ — should be 90× faster. | Actual: only 1.3× due to ~70× utilization penalty from irregular access. | Flash Attention at 8K: ~50% GPU utilization. Sparse: ~1% utilization on the 'saved' FLOPs.

title: Embedding Table Hot-Cold Partitioning
scenario: Your production recommendation system has a 200 GB embedding table serving 100K QPS. Only 5% of embedding rows account for 80% of lookups (Zipfian distribution). You have 80 GB of GPU HBM and 512 GB of CPU DRAM. Design a tiered caching architecture that exploits this access distribution.
common_mistake: Putting the entire table on GPU (doesn't fit) or putting it all in CPU DRAM (too slow for 100K QPS). The Zipfian distribution is the key engineering lever.
realistic_solution: Hot-cold partitioning: Place the hottest 5% of rows (10 GB, covering 80% of lookups) in GPU HBM for near-zero-latency access. Place the remaining 95% (190 GB) in CPU DRAM with a GPU-side cache for recently accessed 'warm' rows. Architecture: (1) Maintain a lookup frequency table; periodically promote/demote rows between tiers. (2) Hot tier (GPU HBM): 10 GB, serves 80% of lookups at HBM bandwidth (2 TB/s). (3) Warm cache (GPU HBM): 20-30 GB LRU cache for frequently accessed 'cold' rows, serving ~15% of lookups. (4) Cold tier (CPU DRAM): 190 GB, serves ~5% of lookups via PCIe (~32 GB/s). The PCI
napkin_math: 100K QPS × 50 lookups × 512B = 2.56 GB/s total read bandwidth. | Hot path (80%): 2.05 GB/s from HBM (trivial at 2 TB/s). | Cold path (5%): 0.13 GB/s via PCIe (trivial at 32 GB/s). | P99 concern: cold lookups add ~10µs PCIe latency vs ~0.3µs HBM.

title: MoE Expert Parallelism vs Tensor Parallelism
scenario: You are deploying a 128-expert MoE model for inference across a cluster. Compare expert parallelism (each GPU holds a subset of experts, tokens routed via AllToAll) vs tensor parallelism (each expert is split across GPUs). Under what conditions does each strategy win?
common_mistake: Defaulting to tensor parallelism because it's familiar from dense models. For MoE, expert parallelism is often superior because it avoids the AllReduce overhead within each expert's computation.
realistic_solution: Expert parallelism (EP): each GPU holds $E/N$ complete experts. Pro: zero communication within expert computation — each GPU runs complete matmuls on its local experts. Con: requires AllToAll to dispatch tokens to the right GPU and collect results. Scales well when experts fit on single GPUs. Tensor parallelism (TP): each expert is split across GPUs. Pro: handles experts too large for one GPU. Con: requires AllReduce within each expert's forward pass — much more frequent synchronization. EP wins when: individual experts fit on one GPU (common for MoE models where each expert is small), batch s
napkin_math: 128-expert model, 16 GPUs: | EP: 8 experts/GPU, 1 AllToAll per MoE layer. AllToAll 4096 tokens × 8KB = 32 MB. | TP-4 on each expert: 4 AllReduces per expert per layer × 8 experts = 32 AllReduces per layer (!). | EP communication: 32 MB/layer. TP: 32 × 32KB = 1 MB/layer but 32× higher latency from synchronization points.

title: Architecture Choice Drives Serving Cost
scenario: You must serve 10,000 QPS of text generation at P99 < 500ms with equivalent quality. Compare the infrastructure cost of serving a dense 70B transformer vs a 47B-parameter MoE (8×7B, top-2) vs a 70B-equivalent SSM. Which architecture minimizes $/query?
common_mistake: Only comparing parameter counts without considering per-token compute, memory requirements, and hardware utilization differences across architectures.
realistic_solution: Dense 70B: 140 GB (BF16) — needs 2× H100 (80GB each) for weights alone, plus KV cache. Per-token FLOPs: ~140 TFLOPs. Memory-bound during decode. MoE 8×7B (top-2): 94 GB weights — also 2× H100, but per-token FLOPs ~26 TFLOPs (13B active). Decode is 5× cheaper in compute, but same memory-bound decode bottleneck (weights still need to be in HBM). The catch: all 47B params must reside in HBM even though only 13B are active per token — poor HBM utilization. SSM 70B-equiv: 140 GB weights, but NO KV cache — fixed-size state (~MBs). Decode is still a weight-limited matmul-per-token, but can batch far
napkin_math: Dense 70B: 2× H100, batch 16 (KV cache limited), ~320 tokens/s. | MoE 47B: 2× H100, batch 16 (weights limited), ~800 tokens/s (5× less compute/token). | SSM 70B: 2× H100, batch 256+ (no KV cache), ~2000+ tokens/s. | At $3/hr per H100: Dense $18.75/M tokens, MoE $7.50/M, SSM $3/M.

title: Long-Context Architecture Selection
scenario: You need to deploy a model that processes 128K-token documents for a legal document analysis product. Your SLA requires processing a full document within 10 seconds on a single 8×H100 node. Compare full attention, sliding window + global tokens, and a hybrid SSM-attention architecture. Which architecture meets the SLA and why?
common_mistake: Assuming full attention can handle 128K tokens efficiently. The quadratic memory and compute make this extremely expensive.
realistic_solution: Full attention at 128K: attention scores = $128K^2 \times 2B \times h$ = massive. Even with Flash Attention, the $O(S^2)$ compute is enormous. On 8× H100: prefill for 128K tokens with a 70B model would take ~30-60 seconds — violates 10s SLA. Sliding window (W=4K) + global tokens: reduces attention to $O(S \cdot W)$, making prefill ~32× faster. 128K/4K = 32× reduction. Prefill: ~1-2 seconds. However, information between distant paragraphs requires many layers to propagate. May miss cross-document references. Hybrid SSM-attention: SSM layers handle the long-range context in $O(S)$ time. A few at
napkin_math: Full attention FLOPs at 128K: $2 \times 128K^2 \times 4096 \times 80$ layers ≈ 10,000 TFLOPs. 8×H100 (8×1000 TFLOPS): ~1.25s just for attention scores (at 100% utilization). Total with FFN: ~30-60s. | Sliding window: $2 \times 128K \times 4K \times 4096 \times 80$ ≈ 340 TFLOPs. ~0.04s. | Hybrid: SSM layers O(S) + few attention layers → ~2-4s total.

title: Edge Vision Model: CNN vs ViT Deployment
scenario: You are deploying a real-time object detection model on a mobile phone with a Qualcomm NPU (8 TOPS INT8, 2 MB SRAM). Compare deploying a MobileNetV2-based detector vs a Mobile ViT model of equivalent accuracy. Which architecture is better suited to this hardware, and why?
common_mistake: Choosing ViT because it's 'newer' without considering the hardware constraints. Mobile NPUs are optimized for regular, channel-wise operations like depthwise separable convolutions — not the dynamic attention patterns in ViT.
realistic_solution: MobileNetV2 wins on this hardware for several reasons: (1) Depthwise separable convolutions map perfectly to NPU hardware (fixed 3×3 or 5×5 kernels, regular memory access). (2) The activation tensors are small at each layer (pooling reduces spatial dimensions progressively), fitting in the 2 MB SRAM. (3) INT8 quantization is straightforward for conv layers (well-studied, minimal accuracy loss). Mobile ViT challenges: (1) Self-attention requires dynamic memory allocation for the $S \times S$ score matrix, which NPU compilers handle poorly. (2) Softmax and LayerNorm require FP16/FP32, forcing mi
napkin_math: MobileNetV2: ~300M FLOPs, peak activations ~1 MB (fits SRAM). On 8 TOPS NPU: ~40ms. | Mobile ViT: ~1 GFLOP, attention scores for 196 patches = 196²×2B×heads = ~150 KB per head (fits SRAM but irregular). Softmax fallback to CPU adds ~10ms. | MobileNetV2: 40ms. Mobile ViT: ~80ms (2× slower on same hardware).

title: DLRM Training Pipeline End-to-End Design
scenario: Design the training infrastructure for a production DLRM at social scale: 12 embedding tables totaling 4 TB, an MLP bottom/top network of 50M parameters, training on 10B examples/day. The system must support hourly model updates. Identify the three most critical systems bottlenecks and how architecture choices address each.

realistic_solution: Bottleneck 1 — Embedding table memory (4 TB): Cannot fit on any single node. Must use model-parallel embedding sharding across ~64+ GPUs (80 GB each). Table-wise sharding with row-wise sharding for oversized tables. AllToAll for cross-table feature interaction. Bottleneck 2 — Sparse gradient communication: Embedding gradients are extremely sparse (only rows accessed in the batch get gradients). Standard AllReduce is wasteful. Use sparse AllReduce or parameter-server-style gradient aggregation. At 10B examples/day with 50 lookups each: ~500B sparse updates/day. Bottleneck 3 — Data pipeline I/O:
napkin_math: 4 TB embeddings / 80 GB per GPU = 50 GPUs minimum just for embeddings. | Data-parallel MLPs: 50M × 16B (BF16 + optimizer) = 800 MB — fits on 1 GPU. | AllToAll per step: batch_size × 12 tables × 128 dim × 2B = batch_size × 3 KB. At batch 64K: 192 MB AllToAll per step. | 10B examples / 86400s = 115K examples/s throughput requirement.

title: Multi-Architecture Serving Fleet Design
scenario: You are the Staff ML Systems engineer responsible for a serving fleet that must run three model types simultaneously: (1) a 175B dense transformer for chat, (2) a 500B MoE model for code generation, and (3) a DLRM with 2 TB embeddings for ads ranking. You have a budget of 256 H100 GPUs. Design the serving architecture, including GPU allocation, parallelism strategy per model, and explain how architecture differences drive different infrastructure decisions.

realistic_solution: Model 1 — Dense 175B Transformer (chat): Needs 350 GB (BF16) → 8-way TP across 8 H100s per replica. KV cache is the batch-size limiter (~2 GB per request at 4K context). Focus: maximize batch size for decode throughput. Allocate 64 GPUs (8 replicas of 8-GPU TP groups). Model 2 — 500B MoE (code gen): ~1 TB total weights but only ~60B active per token. Use 128-way EP (1 GPU per expert) within each replica. Each GPU holds ~8 GB of expert weights, leaving ~70 GB for KV cache → massive batch sizes. Focus: AllToAll latency between experts. Requires NVLink/NVSwitch within each EP group. Allocate 128
napkin_math: Dense 175B: 350 GB / 80 GB = 5 GPUs min (8 for TP alignment). 8 replicas = 64 GPUs. | MoE 500B: 128 experts → 128 GPUs for full EP. ~8 GB weights/GPU. | DLRM 2 TB: 2000 GB / 80 GB = 25 GPUs min → 32 for balance. | Total: 64 + 128 + 64 = 256 GPUs. ✓

title: KV Cache Quantization Quality-Throughput Frontier
scenario: Your 70B LLM serving fleet is KV-cache-limited: at BF16, each H100 can batch only 8 concurrent 8K-context requests. Product wants to 4× the throughput without adding GPUs. Evaluate quantizing the KV cache to INT8, INT4, and FP8 (E4M3). Design a system that maximizes throughput while bounding quality degradation to <1% on your eval suite.
common_mistake: Blindly quantizing all KV cache to INT4 for maximum compression. Different layers and attention heads have different sensitivity to quantization. Uniform quantization often causes disproportionate quality loss in early layers.
realistic_solution: Step 1 — Profile per-layer sensitivity: Run eval suite with per-layer KV cache quantization. Typically, the first 5-10% and last 5-10% of layers are most sensitive (they handle input normalization and output logit formation). Middle layers are robust to aggressive quantization. Step 2 — Mixed-precision KV cache: Keep early/late layers at BF16 or FP8, quantize middle layers to INT4. This gives ~3× compression (not 4×, due to the BF16 layers) with <0.5% quality loss typically. Step 3 — FP8 (E4M3) as compromise: FP8 gives 2× compression with near-zero quality loss (maintains dynamic range better
napkin_math: 70B at 8K context, BF16 KV: ~2.5 GB per request. | 80 GB HBM − 42 GB weights = 38 GB for KV. Batch 8 = 20 GB + overhead. | INT4 KV: ~0.63 GB/request → batch 32 → 4× throughput. | Mixed BF16/INT4: ~0.72 GB/request → batch 28 → 3.5× throughput. | + Paged attention (eliminate 15% fragmentation): batch 32 → 4×. ✓

title: MoE Inference Memory Efficiency Problem
scenario: Your 128-expert MoE model has 800B total parameters but only 12B active per token (top-2 of 128). During inference, all 800B parameters (1.6 TB in BF16) must reside in GPU HBM, but at any given moment only 1.5% of weights are 'hot.' This means 98.5% of your $400K GPU investment is storing cold weights. Design an architecture that dramatically improves memory utilization without degrading latency.
common_mistake: Suggesting CPU offloading — the PCIe roundtrip latency (~10-50µs per expert) would destroy token generation latency. The solution must keep hot experts in HBM while efficiently swapping experts based on routing predictions.
realistic_solution: Speculative expert caching: (1) Pre-routing prediction: Use the gating network's output from the current token to predict which experts the NEXT token will need (gating logits are available before the full forward pass completes). Pre-fetch those experts from a CPU/SSD tier. (2) Expert popularity caching: Maintain a frequency-based cache in GPU HBM. Keep the top-K most frequently accessed experts always resident (this covers 60-70% of requests due to Zipfian expert popularity). (3) Expert fusion: At inference time, merge multiple experts into a single larger expert using weighted averaging bas
napkin_math: 128 experts × 6.25B params × 2B = 1.6 TB total. | Top-2 routing: 2/128 = 1.56% active. | Zipfian: top-16 experts cover ~60% of routes. 16 × 12.5 GB = 200 GB — fits on 3× H100. | Remaining 112 experts: INT4 = 350 GB on 5× H100. | Total: 8× H100 vs 20× H100 for full BF16 — 2.5× cost reduction.

title: The Matrix Multiply FLOP Count
scenario: A single fully connected layer maps a batch of 64 vectors from dimension 4096 to dimension 4096. How many floating-point multiply-accumulate operations (MACs) does this matrix multiplication require, and what is the total FLOP count?
common_mistake: Confusing MACs with FLOPs. Each MAC is one multiply and one add, so 1 MAC = 2 FLOPs. Also forgetting to include the batch dimension in the calculation.
realistic_solution: MACs = B * M * N = 64 * 4096 * 4096 = 1.07 billion MACs. FLOPs = 2 * MACs = 2.15 billion FLOPs (~2.15 GFLOPS). On an A100 with 312 TFLOPS FP16 peak, this single layer takes ~7 microseconds at peak utilization.
napkin_math: MACs = 64 * 4096 * 4096 = 1.07B. FLOPs = 2 * 1.07B = 2.15 GFLOPS. At 312 TFLOPS peak (A100): 2.15e9 / 312e12 = 6.9 us compute floor.

title: Arithmetic Intensity of a Linear Layer
scenario: A linear layer performs Y = XW where X is [B, 4096] and W is [4096, 4096], both in FP16. Calculate the arithmetic intensity of this operation. Is it compute-bound or memory-bound on an A100 GPU (312 TFLOPS FP16, 2 TB/s HBM bandwidth)?
common_mistake: Only counting the weight matrix bytes and forgetting the input and output tensors. Also confusing batch-size-dependent arithmetic intensity with the single-sample case.
realistic_solution: FLOPs = 2 * B * 4096 * 4096. Bytes loaded = B*4096*2 (input) + 4096*4096*2 (weights) + B*4096*2 (output). For B=1: FLOPs = 33.6M, Bytes = 33.6M + 8K + 8K = ~33.6MB. AI = 33.6M / 33.6M = ~1 FLOP/byte (memory-bound). For B=1024: FLOPs = 34.4G, Bytes = 8M + 33.6M + 8M = ~50M. AI = 34.4G / 50M = ~688 FLOPs/byte (compute-bound). The ridge point on A100 is 312T/2T = 156 FLOPs/byte. Batch size drives the transition.
napkin_math: Ridge point = 312 TFLOPS / 2 TB/s = 156 FLOPs/byte. B=1: AI ~1 (memory-bound). B=1024: AI ~688 (compute-bound). Crossover at B ~64.

title: The 3x Rule of Backpropagation
scenario: A colleague claims that the backward pass of a neural network takes roughly 2x the FLOPs of the forward pass, making total training compute per sample approximately 3x the forward pass cost. Explain why this is the case for a stack of linear layers.
common_mistake: Thinking backward is the same cost as forward. Each linear layer's backward pass requires two matrix multiplies (gradient w.r.t. input and gradient w.r.t. weights), while forward requires only one.
realistic_solution: For a linear layer Y = XW, the forward pass is one matmul (2*B*M*N FLOPs). The backward pass computes dX = dY * W^T (one matmul, same size) and dW = X^T * dY (another matmul, same size). So backward = 2x forward FLOPs. Total per training step = 3x forward. For a model doing 10 TFLOPS per forward pass, expect ~30 TFLOPS per training iteration.
napkin_math: Forward: 1 matmul per layer = F FLOPs. Backward: 2 matmuls per layer = 2F FLOPs. Total = F + 2F = 3F. 70B model forward ~140 TFLOPS => training step ~420 TFLOPS.

title: The Activation Memory Explosion
scenario: You are training a 32-layer transformer with hidden dimension 4096, batch size 32, sequence length 2048, in FP16. Each transformer layer must save its input activations for the backward pass. Estimate the total activation memory required across all 32 layers, ignoring attention matrices. Does this fit alongside a 7B FP16 model (14 GB) on a single A100-80GB GPU?
common_mistake: Only counting model weight memory and forgetting that activations scale with batch size and sequence length, often dominating total memory for large batches.
realistic_solution: Per-layer activation tensor shape: [B, S, H] = [32, 2048, 4096] in FP16 (2 bytes). Per-layer memory: 32 * 2048 * 4096 * 2 = 536 MB. Across 32 layers: 536 MB * 32 = ~17.2 GB. Weights: 14 GB. Optimizer states (Adam, FP32): 14 * 2 = 28 GB. Gradients: 14 GB. Total: 14 + 28 + 14 + 17.2 = 73.2 GB. This barely fits on an 80 GB GPU, leaving almost no headroom. Activation checkpointing becomes essential for any larger batch or sequence.
napkin_math: Per layer: 32 * 2048 * 4096 * 2B = 536 MB. 32 layers: 17.2 GB activations. Weights: 14 GB. Adam states: 28 GB. Gradients: 14 GB. Total: ~73 GB / 80 GB. 91% utilization with zero headroom.

title: Gradient Checkpointing: The Memory-Compute Trade
scenario: Your 96-layer LLM training run OOMs on an A100-80GB GPU. Without checkpointing, activation memory is 48 GB. You enable gradient checkpointing, saving activations at every sqrt(96) ~ 10 layers. Estimate the new activation memory and the additional compute cost as a percentage of total training time.
common_mistake: Assuming gradient checkpointing halves memory. It reduces activation memory from O(N) to O(sqrt(N)) layers, but also does not realize the recompute cost is roughly 33% of total training FLOPs, not 100%.
realistic_solution: Without checkpointing: 96 layers of saved activations = 48 GB. With sqrt(96) ~ 10 checkpoints: only ~10 layers of activations stored = ~5 GB. Memory savings: 48 GB -> 5 GB (10x reduction). Compute cost: backward pass recomputes activations for ~86 non-checkpointed layers. Since forward recompute is 1/3 of total training cost (forward = 1/3 total), the overhead is ~(86/96) * (1/3) = ~30% extra wall-clock time. This is the canonical memory-compute tradeoff.
napkin_math: Memory: 48 GB -> ~5 GB (sqrt(N) checkpoints). Compute overhead: recompute 86/96 of forward = ~30% of one forward pass = ~10% of total (fwd+bwd). Practical measurements: 25-35% wall-clock overhead.

title: Forward vs Reverse Mode Autodiff
scenario: A researcher proposes using forward-mode automatic differentiation instead of reverse-mode (backpropagation) for training a transformer with 1 billion parameters and a scalar loss. Why would this be catastrophically expensive? When might forward-mode actually be preferable?
common_mistake: Treating forward-mode and reverse-mode as equivalent in cost. Forward-mode computes one column of the Jacobian per pass; reverse-mode computes one row per pass. When outputs << inputs (scalar loss, billions of params), reverse-mode wins by orders of magnitude.
realistic_solution: Reverse-mode (backprop) computes gradients for ALL 1B parameters in one backward pass (cost: ~2x forward). Forward-mode would require one forward-sensitivity pass per parameter, meaning 1B passes to get the full gradient. Cost ratio: 1B / 1 = 1 billion times more expensive. Forward-mode is preferable when: (1) computing Jacobian-vector products for few inputs (e.g., neural ODE sensitivity), (2) the function has many outputs but few inputs, or (3) computing per-sample gradients without materializing the full Jacobian.
napkin_math: Reverse-mode: 1 backward pass = 2x forward cost = 2F. Forward-mode: 1B passes * 1F each = 1B * F. Ratio: 1B * F / 2F = 500M times slower. Forward-mode only wins when #outputs >> #inputs.

title: The Dying ReLU Diagnosis
scenario: During training of a 12-layer MLP on a cloud GPU, you observe that loss plateaus after 1,000 steps and gradient norms for layers 8-12 drop to near zero. A histogram of layer 10 activations shows 87% of values are exactly zero. What is the root cause, and what are two systems-aware mitigations?
common_mistake: Blaming the optimizer or learning rate without inspecting activation distributions. The 87% zero activations are the direct signature of dying ReLU neurons, which also waste 87% of the compute in those layers.
realistic_solution: Root cause: Dying ReLU. With a large learning rate or poor initialization, weight updates push pre-activation values permanently negative. Once a ReLU neuron dies (output always 0), it receives zero gradient and never recovers. Systems impact: 87% dead neurons means 87% of those layers' MACs are wasted (computing zeros). Mitigations: (1) Switch to LeakyReLU or GELU, which have nonzero gradients for negative inputs (compute cost is identical on modern GPUs). (2) Use batch normalization or layer normalization before the activation, which re-centers pre-activations and prevents the drift into the
napkin_math: 87% dead neurons = 87% wasted FLOPs in those layers. 5 affected layers out of 12 = ~36% of model FLOPs wasted. GELU vs ReLU: same FLOP count on Tensor Cores, 0% dead neurons.

title: The LayerNorm Bandwidth Tax
scenario: A profiler trace of your transformer training shows that LayerNorm operations consume only 0.1% of total FLOPs but account for 8% of wall-clock time. Explain why LayerNorm is so slow relative to its FLOP count, and describe what makes kernel fusion critical here.
common_mistake: Looking only at FLOP count and concluding LayerNorm should be negligible. LayerNorm has extremely low arithmetic intensity, making it purely memory-bandwidth-bound.
realistic_solution: LayerNorm computes mean and variance (reductions), then normalizes and scales (element-wise). For a [B, S, H] tensor, it performs ~5H FLOPs per element but must read the entire tensor from HBM, compute statistics, write back, then read again for normalization. Arithmetic intensity: ~5 FLOPs / (2 * 2 bytes read + 2 bytes write) = ~0.8 FLOPs/byte. At ridge point ~156 FLOPs/byte on A100, this is ~200x below compute-bound. Without fusion, each sub-operation (mean, variance, normalize, scale) is a separate kernel launch with its own HBM round trip: 4 reads + 4 writes. Fusing into one kernel: 1 read
napkin_math: Unfused: 4 HBM round trips * [B*S*H] * 2 bytes. Fused: 1 round trip. For B=32, S=2048, H=4096 in FP16: unfused reads ~2 GB, fused reads ~0.5 GB. At 2 TB/s: 1ms unfused vs 0.25ms fused.

title: Computational Graph Memory Lifetime Analysis
scenario: In a 48-layer transformer, the framework's autograd engine retains every intermediate activation tensor from the forward pass until the corresponding backward computation consumes it. Explain how the memory lifetime pattern differs from a simple stack (LIFO) model, and why this matters for GPU memory allocation fragmentation.
common_mistake: Assuming activations are consumed in strict reverse order of creation (pure LIFO). In practice, skip connections, residual streams, and normalization layers create cross-layer references that prevent simple LIFO deallocation.
realistic_solution: In a pure sequential network, activations would follow a LIFO pattern: layer 48's activations are freed first (consumed in backward), then layer 47, etc. But transformers have residual connections: the input to each layer is added back after the attention and FFN blocks. This means layer N's input activation must survive until layer N's backward pass, but is referenced by both the main path and the residual path. Additionally, LayerNorm saves its input statistics, creating cross-references. The result: activation tensors have overlapping, non-nested lifetimes. This prevents the GPU memory allo
napkin_math: LIFO theoretical peak: activation of 1 layer at a time during backward = 1/48 of total. Actual peak: all 48 layers alive at start of backward = 48x LIFO peak. Fragmentation overhead: 10-20% on top of theoretical. PyTorch caching allocator mitigates but does not eliminate.

title: Vanishing Gradients and Depth Limits
scenario: You are training a 50-layer MLP with sigmoid activations (no skip connections). The gradient at layer 1 is 1e-15 while the gradient at layer 50 is 0.1. Explain the root cause quantitatively using the chain rule, and identify the specific systems consequence of this numerical phenomenon.
common_mistake: Saying 'gradients vanish' without quantifying the exponential decay rate per layer. Also missing the systems consequence: underflowed gradients round to zero in FP16, making the problem qualitatively worse on modern accelerators that use mixed precision.
realistic_solution: Sigmoid's max derivative is 0.25 (at x=0). By the chain rule, the gradient at layer 1 is the product of ~50 Jacobian terms, each bounded by 0.25. Upper bound: 0.25^50 = 7.9e-31. In practice, gradients decay as ~(0.25)^L per layer. From layer 50 to layer 1: 0.1 * 0.25^49 ~ 1e-31, which matches the observation (actual is 1e-15, meaning average per-layer factor is ~(1e-15/0.1)^(1/49) ~ 0.54). Systems consequence: FP16 has minimum subnormal of ~5.96e-8. Any gradient below this rounds to zero, creating dead layers. FP16 cannot represent 1e-15, so layers 1-30 receive exactly zero gradient in mixed p
napkin_math: Sigmoid max derivative: 0.25. Product over 50 layers: 0.25^50 = 7.9e-31. FP16 min subnormal: 5.96e-8. Gradient vanishes to exactly zero in FP16 after ~12 layers. Skip connections: gradient = 1 + f'(x), lower bound 1.0 per layer.

title: The 16x Memory Rule for Training
scenario: A 13B parameter model occupies 26 GB in FP16 for inference. You want to fine-tune it with AdamW on a single GPU. Estimate the total GPU memory required for training at batch size 1, accounting for weights, optimizer states, and gradients. What is the minimum GPU memory tier you need?
common_mistake: Assuming training memory equals model size. Adam maintains two FP32 momentum states per parameter, and gradients must also be stored, creating a massive multiplier over inference-only weight storage.
realistic_solution: Weights (FP16): 13B * 2 bytes = 26 GB. Gradients (FP16): 13B * 2 bytes = 26 GB. Adam FP32 master weights: 13B * 4 bytes = 52 GB. Adam FP32 momentum (m): 13B * 4 bytes = 52 GB. Adam FP32 variance (v): 13B * 4 bytes = 52 GB. Total optimizer + grad: 26 + 26 + 52 + 52 + 52 = 208 GB. Plus activations at batch size 1. This requires either: (1) 3x A100-80GB with FSDP/ZeRO, or (2) a single H100-80GB is insufficient. The 16x multiplier: training needs ~8x the memory of inference weights in FP16 (weights + grads + 3 FP32 copies at 2x each).
napkin_math: Inference: 26 GB. Training (mixed precision AdamW): 26 (weights) + 26 (grads) + 52 (master) + 52 (m) + 52 (v) = 208 GB = 8x inference. With activations: ~210-220 GB total. Need 3x A100-80GB minimum.

title: The Hidden Cost of Dynamic Graphs
scenario: Your team is profiling a PyTorch training loop and notices that CPU utilization is 60% while GPU utilization is only 45%. The nsys trace reveals frequent short gaps between GPU kernel launches. A junior engineer suggests 'the GPU is too slow.' Diagnose the actual bottleneck and propose two concrete solutions.
common_mistake: Blaming the GPU when the symptom is high CPU usage and low GPU usage, which is the classic signature of CPU-side overhead from the Python interpreter and dynamic graph construction starving the GPU.
realistic_solution: The bottleneck is the CPU-side autograd tape construction. In eager mode, PyTorch must: (1) dispatch each operation through the Python runtime, (2) record the operation and its inputs on the autograd tape, (3) allocate metadata, (4) launch the GPU kernel. Each step has Python interpreter overhead (~10-50us per op). For a model with 1000 operations per forward pass, this is 10-50ms of CPU overhead per step. Solutions: (1) torch.compile(): captures the graph statically, eliminating per-operation dispatch overhead and enabling kernel fusion. Expected speedup: 1.5-2x by removing CPU-GPU synchroniz
napkin_math: 1000 ops * 30us dispatch = 30ms CPU overhead per step. GPU compute: 25ms. GPU idle 30/(30+25) = 55% of time waiting for dispatch. torch.compile: eliminates 90% of dispatch => 3ms overhead, GPU utilization jumps to 25/(3+25) = 89%.

title: Skip Connections as Gradient Highways
scenario: Residual connections (skip connections) in ResNet and Transformers add the input to the output: y = f(x) + x. From a gradient flow perspective, why does this simple addition enable training networks with 100+ layers, while networks without skip connections struggle beyond 20 layers?
common_mistake: Explaining skip connections as 'helping the network learn the identity function.' While true, this misses the quantitative gradient flow argument: the +x term provides an additive gradient path that bypasses the multiplicative chain rule decay.
realistic_solution: Without skip connections, the gradient through L layers is a product: grad = prod(dh_i/dh_{i-1}) for i=1..L. If each factor is <1 (common with sigmoid/tanh), this product decays exponentially. With the residual connection y = f(x) + x, the gradient becomes: dy/dx = df/dx + I (identity). The +I term means the gradient through the skip path is always exactly 1.0, regardless of depth. The total gradient is at least 1.0 plus the chain-rule term. This prevents exponential decay and ensures every layer receives a meaningful gradient signal. Systems consequence: this is why transformers can scale to
napkin_math: Without skip: gradient ~ 0.5^L. At L=100: 0.5^100 = 7.9e-31 (zero in FP16). With skip: gradient >= 1.0 at every layer. FP16 can represent 1.0 perfectly. Skip connections turn multiplicative decay into additive composition.

title: Matrix Multiply Tiling for Tensor Cores
scenario: An H100 GPU's Tensor Cores operate on 16x16x16 FP16 matrix tiles. Your model has a linear layer with weight matrix [4096, 4097] -- note the non-power-of-2 dimension. Explain the systems consequence of this shape, quantify the wasted compute, and propose a fix.
common_mistake: Assuming the GPU handles arbitrary shapes efficiently. Tensor Cores require dimensions aligned to tile sizes (multiples of 16). Misaligned dimensions cause padding and wasted compute.
realistic_solution: The Tensor Core processes 16x16x16 tiles. Dimension 4097 requires ceil(4097/16) = 257 tiles along that axis. The last tile pads 4097 to 4112, wasting (4112-4097)/4112 = 0.36% of compute in that dimension. This seems small, but: (1) the padding cascades through all layers referencing this dimension, (2) memory alignment is worse than compute waste -- misaligned memory accesses break coalescing patterns, potentially reducing bandwidth utilization by 2-4x for that layer. Fix: pad the dimension to 4096 (a multiple of 128, which satisfies 16-tile alignment and also aligns with 128-byte cache lines)
napkin_math: 4097 / 16 = 256.06 tiles => 257 tiles * 16 = 4112 columns computed, 15 wasted per tile row. Compute waste: 15/4112 = 0.36%. Memory alignment penalty: potentially 2-4x bandwidth loss. Fix: dim 4096 (multiple of 128). Rule: always use multiples of 128 for hidden dims on NVIDIA GPUs.

title: Designing Mixed-Precision Backpropagation
scenario: You are designing a mixed-precision training pipeline for a 70B LLM. The forward pass runs in FP16 on Tensor Cores. Explain why the loss must be computed in FP32, why a loss scaler is needed, and what happens to the master weights during the optimizer step. Trace the precision of each tensor through the complete forward-backward-update cycle.
common_mistake: Thinking mixed precision just means 'use FP16 everywhere.' The critical insight is that different stages require different precisions, and the loss scaler prevents gradient underflow in the backward pass.
realistic_solution: Precision trace: (1) Forward: inputs and weights cast to FP16. Matrix multiplies execute on Tensor Cores at 2x throughput. Activations saved in FP16. (2) Loss computation: output cast to FP32. Loss computed in FP32 to avoid overflow (softmax cross-entropy can produce values >65,504, FP16 max). (3) Loss scaling: loss multiplied by scale factor (e.g., 2^16 = 65536) in FP32 before backward. This shifts small gradients above FP16's minimum representable value (~6e-8). (4) Backward: gradients computed in FP16 (2x throughput). The scale factor ensures gradients that would be 1e-10 (below FP16 min) b
napkin_math: FP16 max: 65,504. FP16 min subnormal: 5.96e-8. Without loss scaling, any gradient <5.96e-8 rounds to zero. With scale=65536: minimum representable gradient becomes 5.96e-8 / 65536 = 9.1e-13. Master weights in FP32 needed because weight update ~lr*grad ~1e-4 * 1e-3 = 1e-7, which is below FP16 precision for weights ~1.0.

title: Exploiting Activation Sparsity in FFN Layers
scenario: Research shows that ReLU-based transformer FFN layers exhibit ~90% activation sparsity (90% of intermediate activations are zero after ReLU). A team proposes skipping the second matrix multiply for zero-valued rows to achieve near-10x speedup. Evaluate this proposal, identifying both the theoretical opportunity and the hardware obstacles.
common_mistake: Assuming that algorithmic sparsity translates directly to hardware speedup. Unstructured sparsity on dense Tensor Cores yields zero speedup without specialized sparse hardware or software support.
realistic_solution: Theoretical opportunity: if 90% of intermediate activations are zero after ReLU, then 90% of the second matmul (down projection) rows contribute nothing to the output. Skipping them would reduce FLOPs by ~90%, suggesting 10x speedup. Hardware reality: NVIDIA Tensor Cores operate on dense 16x16 tiles. Skipping individual zero rows requires either: (1) Structured N:M sparsity (e.g., 2:4 sparsity on A100/H100): only 2x speedup, requires specific 50% sparsity pattern, not the unstructured 90% pattern we have. (2) Sparse matrix multiplication (cuSPARSE): only faster than dense when sparsity >95-99%
napkin_math: 90% sparse FFN: 10% of rows are nonzero. Dense Tensor Core matmul: 0% savings. 2:4 structured sparse: 2x (requires 50% pattern, not 90% unstructured). Custom compact kernel: 1.5-2x after gather/scatter overhead. Gap between algorithmic opportunity (10x) and hardware reality (1.5-2x) = 5-7x left on the table.
